title: Centrapalus chthonocephalus
taxonomy: Centrapalus chthonocephalus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the daisy family (Asteraceae). It is a member of the genus Centrapalus, which is a group of plants native to Southern Africa. The genus Centrapalus is further classified under the tribe Inuleae, which consists of various daisy-like plants. The plant is closely related to other species in the tribe, such as Inula and Pseudognaphalium. Centrapalus chthonocephalus is a unique species within its classification.
uses: Centrapalus chthonocephalus is a plant species with reported medicinal uses, although more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. The plant has been traditionally used in Africa for its potential antimalarial, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties. It has also been used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. In traditional medicine, the plant's leaves, stems, and roots are often used to prepare infusions and decoctions. Further studies are required to fully understand the potential health benefits and safe uses of Centrapalus chthonocephalus.

intro: Centrapalus chthonocephalus, also known as Cape chthonocephalus, is a plant species belonging to the daisy family (Asteraceae). This plant has been traditionally used in South African folk medicine, offering health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can help soothe wounds and reduce fever. The bioactive constituents of Centrapalus chthonocephalus include sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Cape chthonocephalus is often prepared in the form of infusions or teas, as well as being used in topical creams and ointments to harness its medicinal benefits.
